note_data.title: Work, energy and power
keyPoints: **Work done** is the product of force and displacement in the direction of the force: W = Fs cos θ
**Kinetic energy** is the energy possessed by an object due to its motion: KE = ½mv²
**Gravitational potential energy** is the energy possessed by an object due to its position in a gravitational field: GPE = mgh
**Power** is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = Fv
The **principle of conservation of energy** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another
**Efficiency** is the ratio of useful energy output to total energy input, expressed as a percentage
Work done equals the area under a force-displacement graph
When work is done against friction, kinetic energy is converted to thermal energy
**Elastic potential energy** stored in a spring is given by EPE = ½kx²

definitions.definition: The product of force and displacement in the direction of the force
The rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer
The energy possessed by an object due to its motion
The energy possessed by an object due to its position
The ratio of useful energy output to total energy input

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all working, include units in final answer
Use given data to find the answer with clear method shown

modelAnswer.question: A 2.0 kg mass is lifted vertically through a height of 3.0 m in 5.0 s. Calculate (a) the work done against gravity (b) the minimum power required.
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Work done = mgh = 2.0 × 9.8 × 3.0 = 59 J (b) Power = W/t = 59/5.0 = 12 W

commonMistakes: Never say 'energy is lost' — always say 'energy is transferred to thermal energy' or 'energy is dissipated'
Students often forget to use cos θ when force and displacement are not in the same direction
Confusing power with force — power is energy per second, not force
Using wrong formula for gravitational potential energy — must use mgh, not mgh/2
Forgetting to convert units — especially time to seconds and distances to metres
examinerTips: This topic appears in every paper — especially energy conservation problems
Always check if the question asks for power as rate of work or rate of energy transfer
Show all substitutions clearly in calculations for method marks
Use Pakistani examples: Tarbela Dam converts GPE to electrical energy via turbines
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force-displacement graph showing work done as area under the curve, with force on y-axis and displacement on x-axis
